title: Federal Budget
source: Government of Pakistan, Finance Division — Budget in Brief
dataSource: Finance Division, GoP (as reported by Dawn & Business Recorder budget coverage)
methodologyNote: Budgeted estimates compiled from the Finance Minister's budget speech and the Finance Division's figures as reported in detail by Dawn and Business Recorder. Values are in PKR billion (1,000 bn = 1 tn). Where two reputable sources differ slightly the more detailed figure is used and the discrepancy noted. The Good/Bad/Ugly commentary is editorial opinion, clearly labelled, and grounded in the official figures shown.

years.label: FY2026-27
taxMeasures: Salaried-class relief: 9% surcharge abolished and income-tax rates cut across four upper slabs (e.g. Rs2.2–3.2M slab 23% → 20%).
Super tax abolished for firms earning Rs150M–Rs500M; cut from 10% to 8% for incomes above Rs500M.
Withholding tax on cash withdrawals and on export proceeds reduced (export WHT 2% → 1.25%).
Taxes abolished on sanitary products and contraceptives; duty exemptions on cancer medicines.
Deemed-income tax on property and capital value tax on residents' foreign assets abolished.
IT & freelancer final-tax regime extended to FY2030; 5% WHT introduced on social-media income.
New FED on petroleum solvents and a special excise duty on imported vehicles above 2,000cc.
BISP raised 17% to Rs838bn, expanding coverage toward 12 million families.

commentary.summary: A consolidation-and-relief budget: the salaried class finally gets a break and the primary surplus is preserved, but it leans on a heroic FBR target, an eye-watering defence rise, and a debt-servicing bill that swallows the budget whole.
good: Real relief for the salaried class at last — the 9% surcharge is gone and four upper tax slabs are cut, easing the squeeze on Pakistan's most-documented taxpayers.
Fiscal discipline maintained: a primary surplus of ~2.0% of GDP and an FBR target set 17.6% above the finance minister's pre-year-end FY2025-26 budget-speech estimate support the IMF EFF framework.
Social protection expanded — BISP up 17% to Rs838bn for ~12m families — plus pro-equity moves (no tax on sanitary pads/contraceptives, duty-free cancer medicines).
Pro-growth signals for the digital economy: IT/freelancer final-tax regime extended to FY2030 and export withholding tax cut to 1.25%.
bad: The Rs15.26tr FBR target is optimistic to the point of risk — it is 17.6% above the finance minister's pre-year-end FY2025-26 budget-speech estimate of Rs12.983tr. FBR has not yet published a numeric year-end actual, so the final growth requirement remains uncertain.
Guns over growth: defence leaps to Rs3.0tr (+~18%) while the core federal PSDP is frozen at Rs1.0tr — development is being starved to fund current spending.
Growth target trimmed to 4.0% with inflation pencilled at 8.2% — modest expansion and still-sticky prices mean living standards barely move.
ugly: Debt servicing alone is Rs8.05tr — about 43% of the entire Rs18.8tr budget and larger than all net federal revenue (Rs11.75tr after transfers). Pakistan is borrowing to pay interest on what it already borrowed.

The new 'cooperative federalism' clawback — returning provinces' NFC share above Rs13,350bn to the centre as Article 164 'grants' — is a constitutional grey zone that effectively re-centralises the divisible pool and invites legal and political challenge.
The arithmetic rests on USD 23.4bn of external financing; the budget's credibility hinges on rollovers and friendly-country deposits actually landing.

sources.label: Business Recorder 40425250 — finance minister's pre-year-end FY2025-26 estimate

years.label: FY2025-26
taxMeasures: 18% GST imposed on imported solar panels (later reduced to 10% after public backlash).
New carbon levy of Rs2.5/litre on petroleum products; Petroleum Development Levy target ~Rs1.47tr.
Withholding tax on interest income raised from 15% to 20%; 5% tax on large pension payments.
Defence allocation raised ~20% to Rs2.55tr; BISP funded at Rs716bn.
Agricultural income tax again left to the provinces — no federal AIT (IMF exempted at Pakistan's request).

commentary.summary: The budget that bought the IMF program: a genuine, painful consolidation that cut the deficit to 3.9% and posted a primary surplus — but did it largely on the backs of the salaried class while sparing agriculture and drowning in debt-service.
good: A credible primary surplus (~2.4% of GDP) and the deficit cut to 3.9% from 5.6% — the consolidation that unlocked the IMF Extended Fund Facility.
First steps toward taxing consumption and externalities (carbon levy, higher PDL) rather than only documented incomes.
Continued social protection with BISP funded at Rs716bn despite a tight envelope.
bad: The Rs14.13tr FBR target needed a ~9% one-year jump and leaned again on the salaried class (interest WHT 15% → 20%, 5% tax on big pensions) — squeezing those already in the net.
An 18% GST slapped on imported solar panels taxed the country's brightest spot — the rooftop-solar boom — and had to be walked back to 10% after backlash.
Subsidies cut 14% to Rs1.19tr — fiscally necessary but a risk to the power sector and export competitiveness.
ugly: Debt servicing of Rs8.21tr ≈ 47% of the Rs17.57tr outlay — and roughly equal to the entire NFC transfer to the provinces. It is the single most damning line in the budget.
Agricultural income tax dodged at the federal level yet again — the most under-taxed sector stays protected while salaried workers pay more.
The whole consolidation rests on provinces delivering a ~Rs1.46tr combined cash surplus; Sindh promptly budgeted a deficit, exposing how fragile the national target really is.

sources.label: Dawn 1916292 — FY2025-26 federal budget summary (11 Jun 2025)

execution.note: Consolidated federal + provincial actuals for the first nine months, compared with the same period a year earlier. Nine-month actuals are not directly comparable to the full-year budget totals, but show the trajectory and the fiscal-consolidation story; % of GDP figures are the cleanest comparison.

sources.label: ProPakistani — Pakistan records 27-year-low fiscal deficit (MoF data, 13 May 2026)
The Nation — Fiscal deficit falls below 1pc of GDP for first time
